summary:
The Indus Valley or Harappan civilization, which had flourished across a vast area of the Indian subcontinent from roughly 2600 to 1900 BC, underwent a complex process of decline and transformation during the early second millennium BC. The great urban centers of Mohenjo-daro and Harappa, which had supported populations of tens of thousands with sophisticated water management, standardized weights and measures, and a still-undeciphered writing system, were progressively abandoned as settlement patterns shifted eastward toward the upper Ganges valley and the Indian peninsula. This process of [[event:1848|decline]] was not a single catastrophic collapse but a gradual dispersal extending over several centuries.

Scholars have proposed multiple explanations for the urban decline, including prolonged drought caused by climate shifts around 2200 to 1900 BC, which reduced monsoon rainfall and undermined the agricultural base of the large cities. Tectonic activity may have altered river courses critical to urban water supply. Epidemic disease has also been proposed as a contributing factor. The absence of clear evidence for military conquest, combined with the gradual nature of abandonment, points away from a single catastrophic cause and toward a multi-factor environmental and economic stress.

The aftermath of urban decline did not mean the end of Harappan culture. Late Harappan communities persisted in rural and semi-urban forms across a wide area well into the second millennium BC, maintaining some elements of earlier material culture while adapting to changed conditions. The subsequent [[event:1849|Indo-Aryan migration]] into the subcontinent and the emergence of the [[event:59|Vedic Period]] represent a new cultural layer overlying the transformed remnants of the Harappan world, the precise relationships between which remain an active area of archaeological and genetic research.
